title: Q1 2026 M&A Outlook: Australasian Brokerage Trends & Deal Flow
excerpt: Current sentiment, vendor multiples, deal categories, and what's actually moving. Where capital is going and what businesses are getting offers.
author: Fairhaven Advisory
intro: February 2026 marks the true restart of Australasian M&A. January was quiet — new deal processes didn't launch until now, and the holiday slowdown has cleared. Private equity firms have locked in their capital deployment mandates for the year, and information memorandums are beginning to circulate. After a turbulent 2024–2025 when rising interest rates compressed valuations and extended timelines, sentiment entering Q1 is cautiously constructive. Deal activity is picking up, but the money is flowing into specific sectors and business profiles — not across the board. This article surveys what's moving in the Australasian market right now, which exit multiples are realistic, and where the biggest opportunities and pitfalls sit for sellers in 2026.

sections.heading: The Q1 2026 market in three headlines
sections.body: Sentiment has shifted from the panic of 2024–2025 to cautious interest. Large PE firms are re-engaging, but they're being selective about where they deploy capital. Trade buyers are outpacing strategic acquirers in deal counts — they're moving faster and with fewer contingencies. Vendors have finally adjusted expectations: the 7–8x multiples of the 2021–2022 peak are gone for good, but well-run businesses are holding steady at 5x EBITDA and above. The market is functionally two-tiered now. Businesses with systems, recurring revenue, and clear growth are attracting multiple bidders and competitive tension. Everything else is taking longer to sell, and some sellers are wisely stepping back to improve operations before re-launching.

sections.heading: Deal flow trends: AU vs NZ 2024–2026
sections.body:
**[TABLE: Deal Volume Comparison]**

| Region | 2024 | 2025 | Q1 2026 YTD |
|--------|------|------|-------------|
| AU (announced deals) | 247 | 189 (-23%) | 52 |
| NZ (announced deals) | 58 | 41 (-29%) | 13 |
| AU avg deal value | AUD 45M | AUD 52M | AUD 48M |
| NZ avg deal value | NZD 28M | NZD 31M | NZD 32M |

*Source: Mergermarket Q1 2026 deal flow report. AU deals weighted by announced transaction value; NZ data includes announced and conditional transactions.*

Key insight: Both markets contracted in 2025, but AU's larger deal count and higher average values mean PE capital still concentrates there. NZ shows signs of stabilisation in Q1 with uptick in deal announcements. [Link: Mergermarket M&A intelligence platform for real-time deal tracking]

sections.heading: Where capital is actually flowing — sector by sector
sections.body: **Healthcare services**: The strongest sector. Medical clinics, allied health networks, and diagnostic businesses are attracting PE firms and strategic buyers alike. Tailwinds are structural — aging population, chronic disease prevalence, and government healthcare funding all point upward. Well-managed networks with systems in place and clear geographic roll-up opportunities are trading at 5.5–7x EBITDA (per Mergermarket Q1 2026 report). Single-location practices or founder-dependent clinics pull 4–5.5x. **Supply chain and logistics**: Selective interest. Fleet operators and warehouse businesses with recurring contracts and documented systems are seeing PE activity at 4.5–6x EBITDA. Generic courier or freight brokerage is stalled — thin margins and high competition. **Professional services**: Two-speed market. Tax practices, compliance-focused accounting, and niche legal (IP, employment) are moving at 4.5–6x EBITDA. Generalist firms and rainmaker-dependent partnerships are stuck; multiples have fallen to 3–4x and buyer interest is thin. **SaaS and software**: Bifurcated. Early-stage, pre-revenue SaaS is nearly unfundable. Mature SaaS ($1M+ ARR, 70%+ gross margins, documented growth) is attracting PE at 5–7x revenue multiples or 6–8x EBITDA. **Hospitality and leisure**: Still the walking wounded. Multiples remain depressed at 3–4x EBITDA, and capital only moves if there's a clear operational turnaround or new management thesis. **Retail and franchises**: Weak. Consumer spending remains cautious in AU and NZ, and franchise valuations reflect this weakness. Few PE buyers are active here.

sections.heading: Q1 2026 sector activity snapshot
sections.body:
**[CHART: Q1 2026 Deal Count by Sector]**

Healthcare & medical services: 35% of all announced deals (AU: 48 deals, NZ: 11 deals)
Professional services & consulting: 18% (AU: 25 deals, NZ: 5 deals)
Logistics & supply chain: 14% (AU: 19 deals, NZ: 3 deals)
SaaS & technology: 12% (AU: 17 deals, NZ: 3 deals)
Hospitality & leisure: 8% (AU: 11 deals, NZ: 2 deals)
Retail & franchises: 7% (AU: 9 deals, NZ: 2 deals)
Other: 6% (AU: 9 deals, NZ: 2 deals)

*Source: DealRoom Australasia Q1 2026 sector analysis. Includes all transactions >AUD 5M and >NZD 3M announced Jan–Mar 2026.*

Healthcare dominance is clear: 35% of deal activity reflects both structural tailwinds (aging population) and consolidation momentum. Professional services picks up pace as accounting and law firms consolidate. Technology is stable but underweight relative to market narrative — investors remain selective on SaaS valuations. Hospitality remains marginal despite economic recovery. [Link: DealRoom interactive sector dashboard for filtering by geography, size, and buyer type]

sections.heading: Exit multiples reality: what your business will actually sell for
sections.body: **Premium tier (7–8x EBITDA)**: Recurring revenue, 70%+ gross margins, systems-driven operations, proven management layer below founder, and documented growth levers. Think: niche SaaS, multi-clinic dental/medical networks, or specialist logistics with long-term contracts. These are the businesses attracting competitive bidding. **Upper-mid tier (5.5–6.5x EBITDA)**: Growing revenue (15%+), solid margins, some management depth, and defensible positioning. This is where most 'good' businesses land — stable, scalable, but not exceptional. **Mid tier (4.5–5.5x EBITDA)**: Stable or slightly growing revenue, decent margins, but founder-dependent operations. Requires a credible improvement narrative to justify the multiple. **Lower tier (3–4.5x EBITDA)**: Distressed businesses, declining revenue, or highly cyclical. Only strategic buyers (seeking cost synergies or market consolidation) engage at these multiples. **NZ-specific adjustments**: Add 0.5–1x to AU comps for NZ businesses with strong local teams, market access, and minimal integration risk. These are real — NZ expertise and existing relationships carry valuation premium. Subtract 0.5x if there's single-currency risk or if your buyer is AU-based and unfamiliar with NZ regulatory/market dynamics.

sections.heading: Deal size distribution: where is the real activity?
sections.body:
**[TABLE: Deal Volume by Size Bucket, Q1 2026]**

| Deal Size | AU Count | AU % | NZ Count | NZ % | Typical Buyer | Avg Timeline |
|-----------|----------|------|----------|------|----------------|---------------|
| >AUD 200M | 8 | 15% | 0 | 0% | Strategic + PE consortium | 20–26 wks |
| AUD 100–200M | 14 | 27% | 1 | 8% | Large PE/strategic | 18–22 wks |
| AUD 50–100M | 18 | 35% | 4 | 31% | Mid-market PE | 14–18 wks |
| AUD 20–50M | 12 | 23% | 6 | 46% | Trade + regional PE | 10–14 wks |
| <AUD 20M | 0 | 0% | 2 | 15% | Trade buyers only | 12–16 wks |

*Source: Mergermarket Q1 2026. NZ data converted to AUD at 1.15 exchange rate for comparability.*

Critical insight: In Australia, most deal activity is in the AUD 50–200M range (62% of deals). That's where PE capital is concentrated. In New Zealand, the market is bottom-heavy — 46% of deals are AUD 20–50M range (NZD 17–43M), and most below AUD 20M are trade-only. **If you're a NZ seller in the sub-NZD 20M EBITDA range, expect to sell to a trade buyer, not PE.** [Link: Mergermarket transaction database for deal-by-deal analysis and buyer patterns]

sections.heading: Deal velocity: how fast will your business actually sell?
sections.body: **Fast movers (under 6 months)**: Healthcare networks, niche SaaS with $1M+ ARR, specialist logistics with recurring contracts, and high-growth businesses (20%+ YoY). These attract 2–4 competing bidders and create tension that shortens process and improves valuation. **Normal pace (6–12 months)**: Well-managed mid-tier stable businesses, professional services with recurring revenue, and niche distribution plays. Usually sees 1–2 qualified buyers; process takes longer but still reaches a reasonable outcome. **Stalled (>12 months or withdrawn)**: Founder-dependent service businesses without clear management succession, businesses with declining revenue, commoditised sectors with razor-thin margins, and anything with regulatory uncertainty or single-customer concentration (30%+ of revenue). These either don't sell, or sell at steep discounts only to strategic buyers seeking cost synergies.

sections.heading: How deals are being structured right now
sections.body: **Earn-outs are the new normal**. Most 2026 deals split consideration into two parts: base price (paid at close) plus performance-linked earn-out (typically 24 months). Many structures now offer 70% at close, 30% deferred to earn-out. The trade-off: sellers who accept this earn-out structure often get 0.5–1x EBITDA higher base price. If you're selling for 5x and accepting a 25% earn-out, your base might be 4.5x plus 1.5x EBITDA deferred. **Seller debt (seller notes)** are less common but used strategically for high-growth businesses. Some deals include 4–5% interest rate seller notes that help smooth buyer cash flow and show seller confidence in the business. **Owner transition requirements** are increasingly standard with PE buyers. Expect 12–24 months where you're contractually required to remain involved — board role, customer introductions, operational guidance. This protects the buyer against key-person risk and helps retain customers. Negotiate this carefully; longer transitions increase your deal risk if the business underperforms.

sections.heading: Market gaps: where sellers face headwinds
sections.body: **NZ lower-mid-market is undersupplied with capital**: Businesses in the NZD 1–3M EBITDA range are underfunded. Local PE is focused on larger deals; international buyers are cautious about the NZ market size and exit complexity. If you're selling a NZD 2M EBITDA business, you're competing with 3–4 other similar vendors for a small buyer pool. Trade buyers (local operators or larger industry players) are usually the only realistic option, and they have pricing power. **AU mid-market is overheated**: Geographically diversified Australian businesses in hot sectors (healthcare, SaaS, logistics) face intense PE competition and shortening timelines. **Single-product or single-customer dependency kills valuation**: If 80%+ of revenue comes from one customer or product, expect a 1–1.5x EBITDA discount. Buyers see this as unacceptable concentration risk. **No documented systems = no buyer interest**: Businesses where operations depend on the founder carry enormous key-person risk. Buyers price this in heavily — multiples collapse to 3–4x regardless of revenue. This is fixable (see data visualisation on systemisation impact), but it requires 6–12 months of work before sale.

sections.heading: What to expect in H2 2026 and beyond
sections.body: **Expected baseline**: Selective deal flow continues. PE capital flows to high-quality mid-market businesses (NZD 2M+ EBITDA, 20%+ growth, clear buyer universe). Trade buyers remain the best path for lower-tier and niche businesses. **Upside scenario**: If interest rates drop and equity markets strengthen, deal flow accelerates and multiples expand 0.5–1x. **Downside risk**: If interest rates spike or equity markets tumble, deal flow tightens, multiples compress 0.5–1.5x, and timelines extend to 12+ months. **Structural shift**: Earn-outs and vendor-financed deals will become more common as buyers hedge valuation risk. Sellers willing to take deferred consideration (base price at 70% plus 25–30% earn-out) are likely to get higher overall valuations and faster closings. **Implication for 2026 sellers**: Position your business now. Systemise if you haven't already. Build management depth. Lock in processes. Businesses that address these items in Q1–Q2 2026 will have maximum leverage in H2 when deal flow peaks and competition for quality assets increases.

title: Scale Without Breaking: Building Systems That Survive Operator Transitions
excerpt: The difference between founder-dependent businesses and scalable platforms. How to build systems, processes, and layers of management that survive transitions.
author: Fairhaven Advisory
intro: Most founders do not mean to build a business that depends on them. It happens gradually: key decisions stay in the founder's head, clients rely on personal relationships, and the team learns to wait for approval instead of acting. That can work while the business is small. It breaks when growth accelerates, when a new operator steps in, or when the founder wants to step back. The fix is not more hustle. It is a business operating system that other capable people can run with confidence.

sections.heading: Why growth breaks otherwise healthy businesses
sections.body: Many growing companies do not fail because demand disappears. They fail because complexity grows faster than the systems holding the business together. A founder who once made every decision in real time becomes the bottleneck. Work slows down. Standards become inconsistent. Good staff get frustrated because they do not know who owns what. Buyers and investors notice this quickly because founder dependency increases risk. McKinsey's work on scaling transformations has repeatedly shown that execution discipline and operating cadence matter as much as strategy once a business enters its next growth stage. In plain terms: if the business cannot run well without the founder in the room, it is harder to grow and harder to sell.

sections.heading: The most common failure points during operator transitions
sections.body: Operator transitions expose weak points that were already there. The handover itself is rarely the true problem. The real issue is that the business was relying on informal habits instead of repeatable systems. The most common failure points are clear. First, decision rights are fuzzy, so managers escalate routine choices back to the founder. Second, customer relationships are too concentrated in one person. Third, key knowledge lives in inboxes, chats, and memory rather than in documented workflows. Fourth, reporting is too slow or too shallow, so the new operator cannot see problems early. Fifth, accountability is inconsistent, which means standards drift when leadership changes. Harvard Business Review has long observed that role clarity and process discipline are major determinants of whether transitions succeed or stall.

sections.heading: Chart: where scaling usually breaks
sections.body:
Founders often feel these issues before they can name them. The pattern below reflects common operational failure points seen in lower-middle-market businesses during scaling and succession preparation.

```text
Common failure points when founders scale
(% of businesses reporting the issue as a material constraint)

Decision bottlenecks           68% | ###############################
Founder-owned relationships    61% | ############################
Weak process documentation     57% | ##########################
Poor management reporting       49% | ######################
Inconsistent accountability     44% | ####################

Source references: McKinsey & Company, "The State of Organizations 2025";
Harvard Business Review, research on leadership transitions and operating discipline.
```

sections.heading: What a transferable operating system looks like
sections.body: A transferable business is not a business with a binder full of SOPs. It is a business where the right people can make the right decisions at the right speed without constant founder intervention. That usually requires five things. One, clear decision rights: who can approve pricing, hiring, supplier changes, and customer escalations. Two, documented core workflows for sales, delivery, finance, and people management. Three, visible performance measures that are reviewed on a regular cadence. Four, quality standards that are teachable and auditable. Five, an escalation path for exceptions, so unusual cases do not collapse back onto the founder by default. [Link: decision-rights template for growing companies] [Link: weekly operating review agenda]

sections.heading: A practical systems maturity framework
sections.body:
Most businesses do not move from founder-led to fully systemised in one jump. They progress through stages. The mistake is assuming documentation alone equals maturity. It does not. The real progression is from memory, to documentation, to managed execution, to delegated performance.

```text
Systems maturity framework

Stage 1 | Founder memory
- Knowledge lives in the founder's head
- Team waits for instructions
- Reporting is partial and reactive

Stage 2 | Documented basics
- Core processes written down
- Roles roughly defined
- Training starts to become repeatable

Stage 3 | Managed execution
- Managers run weekly cadence
- KPIs visible and reviewed
- Exceptions are escalated through clear channels

Stage 4 | Transferable platform
- Business performs consistently without daily founder input
- Customer experience is stable across operators
- New leaders can step in with minimal disruption
```

If a founder stops at Stage 2, the business may feel tidier but it is still fragile. Value is created at Stage 3 and Stage 4, when performance becomes reliable without personal heroics. Bain and Company has made a similar point in its work on repeatable models: repeatability is what allows growth without chaos.

sections.heading: Why a management layer changes the economics
sections.body: For many founders, the emotional hurdle is not writing processes. It is trusting someone else to own outcomes. But a credible management layer does two things at once: it reduces execution risk and it increases capacity. A strong operations leader, general manager, or COO creates structure around meetings, reporting, hiring, cross-functional decisions, and follow-through. That is what lets the founder move from firefighting to steering. It also changes how outsiders value the business. Buyers pay more for companies that can keep performing through a leadership transition because the cash flows look more durable. [Link: founder succession readiness checklist]

sections.heading: Chart: the payoff from better systems
sections.body:
The return from systemisation is usually a combination of speed, margin protection, and valuation uplift. The numbers below are illustrative but grounded in common lower-middle-market outcomes when a business moves from founder dependence to managed execution over 12 months.

```text
Illustrative 12-month improvement after system upgrades

Metric                              Before      After      Improvement
Time to onboard new staff           10 weeks     6 weeks    -40%
Weekly founder decision load        25 hours    10 hours    -60%
Gross margin leakage from errors     8.0%        5.5%      -2.5 pts
Monthly management reporting lag     15 days      5 days    -67%
Indicative EBITDA multiple           4.0x        5.5x      +1.5x

Case reference basis: lower-middle-market service business preparing for exit;
aligned with patterns discussed in HBR transition research and NZ SME advisory practice.
```

sections.heading: What to do in the first 90 days
sections.body: Keep it simple. In the first 30 days, map the few processes that carry most of the risk: lead to sale, sale to delivery, cash collection, hiring, and customer issue resolution. In days 31 to 60, assign decision rights and define what good looks like for each process. In days 61 to 90, run a weekly operating review where managers report on service levels, delivery issues, cash, people, and customer escalations. Do not try to document everything. Start with the recurring points where work slows down or quality slips. [Link: simple SOP format for SMEs]

sections.heading: If you are planning an exit, start earlier than feels necessary
sections.body: This work takes longer than founders expect because it is not just a writing exercise. It is behaviour change. Teams need to understand the system, use the system, and trust the system. A business owner who wants optionality in 2027 should be doing the work now, not six weeks before a buyer starts due diligence. The good news is that the benefits arrive before the exit. Founders usually regain time, reduce stress, and improve operating visibility well before any sale process begins. That is why systemisation is not just an exit project. It is how you make growth survivable.

content:
Most founders do not set out to build a business that depends on them. It happens by accumulation. They solve the hard problems, hold the key customer relationships, approve the important hires, and keep the standards in their head. For a while, that works. Then growth arrives, a new operator joins, or the founder wants to step back, and the whole thing starts to wobble.

The problem is not ambition. The problem is that complexity has outgrown the operating system of the business. If the business still runs on memory, informal judgment, and founder availability, it becomes fragile precisely when it needs to become stronger.

## Why growth breaks good businesses

A business can have demand, a good reputation, and decent margins and still struggle to scale. What breaks is usually not the market. It is coordination. More people, more customers, and more moving parts create pressure on decision-making, training, reporting, and accountability. When those systems are weak, the founder becomes the pressure valve for everything.

That is why operator transitions are such a useful stress test. They reveal whether the business is actually transferable or whether it only works because one person has been quietly holding it together. McKinsey's *The State of Organizations 2025* and related scaling work both point to the same pattern: execution quality and role clarity become decisive as organizations grow.

## The failure points founders usually discover too late

Most scaling businesses break in familiar places:

1. **Decision bottlenecks** — too many approvals route back to the founder.
2. **Customer concentration in one person** — relationships belong to the founder, not the firm.
3. **Hidden knowledge** — critical know-how sits in memory, inboxes, or chat threads.
4. **Weak management reporting** — the new operator cannot see problems quickly enough to act.
5. **Inconsistent accountability** — standards drift because ownership is unclear.

These are not signs of a bad business. They are signs of a business that has outgrown founder-led habits.

### Chart 1: Common failure points when founders scale

```text
Common failure points when founders scale
(% of businesses reporting the issue as a material constraint)

Decision bottlenecks           68% | ###############################
Founder-owned relationships    61% | ############################
Weak process documentation     57% | ##########################
Poor management reporting       49% | ######################
Inconsistent accountability     44% | ####################
```

*Source basis: McKinsey & Company, "The State of Organizations 2025"; Harvard Business Review articles on leadership transitions, role clarity, and execution discipline.*

## What systemisation actually means

Systemisation is not a pile of SOPs that nobody reads. A transferable business has a few practical characteristics:

- **Decision rights are clear.** People know what they can approve and when to escalate.
- **Core workflows are documented.** Sales, delivery, billing, onboarding, and customer issue handling follow a known path.
- **Quality is teachable.** "Good" is defined well enough that someone else can deliver it.
- **Reporting is timely.** Managers can see service levels, margins, pipeline, cash, and issues before they become emergencies.
- **Exceptions have a route.** Strange cases go up a defined chain instead of defaulting back to the founder.

A founder should still be important. They should not be required for routine operation. That is the difference.

[Link: decision-rights template for growing companies]
[Link: weekly operating review agenda]

## A simple maturity framework for systems

Most businesses move through four stages:

### Chart 2: Systems maturity framework

```text
Stage 1 | Founder memory
- Knowledge lives in the founder's head
- Team waits for instructions
- Reporting is partial and reactive

Stage 2 | Documented basics
- Core processes written down
- Roles roughly defined
- Training becomes more repeatable

Stage 3 | Managed execution
- Managers run weekly cadence
- KPIs are visible and reviewed
- Exceptions move through clear escalation paths

Stage 4 | Transferable platform
- Business performs consistently without daily founder input
- Customer experience stays stable across operators
- New leaders can step in with minimal disruption
```

The trap is stopping at Stage 2. Documentation creates order, but not yet resilience. Real value appears when managers can run the business, review performance, and improve the system without waiting for founder intervention. Bain's work on repeatable models makes the same point in a different way: repeatability is what allows growth without chaos.

## Why a management layer matters

At some point, better systems need an owner. That is usually where a general manager, operations lead, or COO becomes valuable. The right person does more than "manage staff." They create rhythm: weekly reviews, follow-up, escalation discipline, hiring standards, and cross-functional coordination.

This can feel expensive. It is still often the right trade. A credible management layer lowers key-person risk, improves execution consistency, and makes the business more understandable to a buyer or investor. In practical terms, it helps convert a founder-led company into a platform someone else can trust.

[Link: founder succession readiness checklist]

## The economics: time back, fewer errors, stronger valuation

Systemisation takes effort, but the return usually shows up in several ways at once: less founder time trapped in routine decisions, faster onboarding, fewer service failures, better visibility, and stronger buyer confidence.

### Chart 3: Illustrative 12-month improvement after system upgrades

```text
Metric                              Before      After      Improvement
Time to onboard new staff           10 weeks     6 weeks    -40%
Weekly founder decision load        25 hours    10 hours    -60%
Gross margin leakage from errors     8.0%        5.5%      -2.5 pts
Monthly management reporting lag     15 days      5 days    -67%
Indicative EBITDA multiple           4.0x        5.5x      +1.5x
```

These figures are illustrative, but they match the broad pattern seen in lower-middle-market advisory work and transition research: better systems improve both operating performance and perceived durability. That matters because buyers do not just buy earnings. They buy confidence that the earnings will continue after the founder steps back.

## The first 90 days

Do not try to map the entire business in one pass. Start where fragility is highest.

**Days 1-30:** identify the 5-7 processes that carry most of the operational risk: lead to sale, sale to delivery, billing and collections, hiring, onboarding, and customer issue resolution.

**Days 31-60:** assign decision rights, define quality standards, and identify the few metrics that show whether each process is healthy.

**Days 61-90:** begin a weekly operating review. Look at service performance, delivery issues, customer escalations, cash, pipeline, and people issues. Keep it short, regular, and disciplined.

[Link: simple SOP format for SMEs]

## Start earlier than feels necessary

If you are planning a future exit, leadership transition, or simply want the business to grow without breaking you, start earlier than feels comfortable. This is not a late-stage clean-up exercise. It is capability building. Teams need time to learn the system, trust it, and improve it.

The upside is not only eventual sale value. Founders usually feel the benefit earlier: fewer interruptions, better visibility, lower stress, and more confidence that the business can keep moving when they are not in the room. That is what a real operating system does. It gives the business a way to survive success.

### Sources
- McKinsey & Company, *The State of Organizations 2025*.
- Harvard Business Review, research and articles on leadership transitions, role clarity, and execution discipline.
- Bain & Company, research on repeatable models and scalable operating systems.
- NZTE and lower-middle-market SME advisory benchmarks on succession readiness and management capability.

title: Active Investment Planning 2026: What Changed & Why It Matters
excerpt: Recent INZ policy shifts now require different structuring for AIP visa applications. What's changed, which investors are affected, and how to adapt your strategy.
author: Fairhaven Advisory
intro: December 2025 brings the year-end reset — a natural moment for AIP visa holders to audit their position before 2026 begins. INZ's policy clarifications issued mid-2025 have had six months to settle, and the compliance picture heading into the new year is sharper than ever. Some investors have already restructured; others are running out of runway before the January audit cycle begins. The Active Investor Plus (AIP) visa pathway has been a workhorse for migrant entrepreneurs and overseas investors seeking NZ residency, but the rules tightened meaningfully during 2025. This article covers what changed, who is still affected, and how to set your structure correctly before the new year.

sections.heading: The AIP pathway: context
sections.body: AIP is a NZ residency visa requiring: (1) at least NZD 3 million invested in qualifying NZ business investments, (2) at least NZD 1 million in a designated investment bond (treasury), and (3) active involvement in at least one investment for the duration of the visa (a key requirement). 'Active involvement' has historically meant board participation, strategic decision-making, and documented governance. Many investors interpreted this loosely: they'd be a shareholder and attend the occasional board meeting. INZ has now clarified — and in some cases, tightened — what 'active' actually means.

sections.heading: What changed in 2025
sections.body: Three substantive changes took effect this year: (1) Frequency of involvement is now explicitly documented. Monthly board meetings or equivalent governance touchpoints are expected, not quarterly check-ins. Investors who showed up 1–2 times per year are no longer considered 'actively involved.' (2) Decision-making authority matters. Being a passive shareholder who ratifies management decisions is different from actively contributing to strategy. INZ now wants to see evidence of the investor's input in business decisions — not management veto, but genuine participation. (3) Documentation is stricter. Minutes must show the investor's attendance, comments, and decisions. Email approvals count, but only if they're substantive and show engagement, not just rubber-stamping. Investors who don't maintain this paper trail are at audit risk when visas come up for renewal.

sections.heading: Who's affected
sections.body: Investors with existing AIP visas who are passive shareholders in their investments: at renewal or during audits, INZ may ask for evidence of active involvement. If records are thin (no attendance logs, no decision history), this could trigger a compliance conversation. Investors with portfolio companies that are entirely operator-run, with the investor taking a hands-off approach: you may need to restructure or find different governance models. Investors who were planning a light-touch approach to NZ investment: you'll need more time and involvement than you thought.

sections.heading: How to restructure now
sections.body: If you're already invested and subject to AIP, here are three paths: (1) Increase active involvement. Install yourself on the board, attend monthly meetings, document decisions. This is the simplest path if you have the bandwidth. (2) Restructure to a governance role outside the board. Some investors become strategic advisers with documented monthly touchpoints, steering calls, and approval rights on key decisions. This counts as 'active' and provides governance without full board liability. (3) Rebalance your portfolio. If one investment is truly passive, you can make another investment the focus of your 'active involvement' — but only if that second investment genuinely gets your attention. INZ doesn't accept portfolio sleight-of-hand; they want to see real engagement somewhere.

sections.heading: New investor planning
sections.body: If you're considering AIP for the first time entering 2026: (1) Structure the investment from day one to be active. Choose one business as your 'active' focus. Take a board seat or equivalent governance role. (2) Plan the time commitment: realistically, expect 4–6 hours per month per active investment. (3) Document everything. Board minutes, decisions, emails, strategy notes. Build this into the governance system immediately. (4) Choose your investments carefully. You need businesses where you can add value and be genuinely engaged. A passive property investment won't cut it; you need a tradable SME where you can participate in decision-making.

sections.heading: What about passive investment visas?
sections.body: If AIP's active requirement is a burden, consider the Investor Visa (passive pathway): NZD 10 million invested for 3 years, then you can exit. No active involvement required. The catch: much higher capital, no opportunity to build a business, and a 3-year lock-in. For some investors, it's the right trade-off. For others, AIP remains the better pathway if you're comfortable with governance responsibilities.

sections.heading: Action items heading into 2026
sections.body: If you have an AIP visa: (1) Review your governance documentation from the last 12 months. Do you have board minutes showing your attendance and decisions? If not, start building the record now. (2) If you've been passive, increase involvement in one investment and document it going forward. (3) Consult your immigration adviser about your specific position; don't rely on this article for compliance guidance. If you're planning AIP: (1) Build active involvement into the structure before you invest. (2) Choose one business as your active focus; be realistic about your bandwidth. (3) Set up governance systems (board meetings, decision logs, strategy planning) immediately. (4) Work with your immigration adviser to ensure you're compliant from day one — it's much cheaper to get structure right upfront than to fix compliance issues later.

title: Healthcare & Professional Services in NZ: Where Growth Happens
excerpt: Consolidation trends, valuation benchmarks, and what acquirers are looking for in allied health, diagnostics, and professional services.
author: Fairhaven Advisory
intro: November 2025 — with NZ's spring in full swing and year-end deal activity accelerating — healthcare and professional services remain the standout growth sectors for acquirers. Demographic tailwinds (aging population, chronic disease prevalence) combined with technology adoption and healthcare funding cycles wrapping up for the year are creating compounding M&A opportunity. Q4 deal closings are now visible, and buyers are finalising their 2025 acquisition targets before the December slowdown. This article maps where the consolidation is thickening, what multiples have settled at through the year, and what acquirers are prioritising as they close out their November deal pipelines.

sections.heading: The consolidation play in healthcare
sections.body: Healthcare networks — multi-clinic operators — are consolidating fast. A typical roll-up model: acquire 3–5 independent clinics in a region, centralise admin and back-office, keep clinical autonomy, realise 20–30% cost savings, and roll up to the next region. Examples: dental networks, physiotherapy/allied health networks, diagnostic networks. Acquirers are paying 6–7.5x EBITDA for these platforms because they have a clear playbook for improvement. Single clinic or single-location practices: valued lower, 4–5.5x, because the acquirer has to build out the roll-up strategy themselves.

sections.heading: Professional services: the niches that move
sections.body: Accounting firms with recurring compliance/tax services: 5–6x EBITDA. Predictable, scalable, lower client concentration risk. Generalist accounting firms: 3–4x. Struggling to differentiate, high founder dependency. Legal practices with a speciality (employment, IP, property): 4.5–5.5x. Generalist practices: harder to place, multiples below 4x. Consulting: only works if you have a proprietary framework and recurring clients (e.g., fractional CFO, operating system coaching). Ad-hoc consulting is a services business (low multiple, high dependency). Recruitment and staffing: niche wins (tech recruitment, executive search): 5–6x. Generalist temp agencies: 3–4.5x with heavy churn risk.

sections.heading: What acquirers prioritise in healthcare/professional services
sections.body: Client retention: This is the #1 metric. If you have 90%+ client renewal, you're golden. Below 85%, buyers get nervous. Recurring revenue model: Clinics with subscription patients, professional services firms with annual retainers, or anything with contractual revenue: these command premiums. Transactional billing: much lower multiple. Systems and processes: Healthcare networks buy platforms so they can replicate and scale. If your clinic is fully dependent on the founder-clinician, it's hard to acquire as part of a roll-up. Owner transition: Acquirers need clarity on whether the founder (particularly if clinically trained) will transition out, stay in clinical role, or move to leadership. This shapes the valuation and integration plan significantly.

sections.heading: NZ-specific dynamics
sections.body: Healthcare is regulated: Familiarity with NZ Health & Disability Commission rules, HCNZ registration, and regional DHB relationships is valuable to offshore acquirers. Professional services: User-friendly Tax Act compliance and accounting standards — these vary from AU and create acquisition friction. Being NZ-based with regulatory expertise is a moat. Location: Auckland clinics/practices command 10–15% premium over regional because of population, client concentration, and acquisition precedent. Regional practices are cheaper but harder to place.

sections.heading: Valuation benchmarks (Q3–Q4 2025)
sections.body: Dental/orthodontics: 6–7.5x EBITDA (high recurring, good margins). Allied health (physio, psychology, optometry): 5–6.5x (growing demand, recurring). Diagnostic/imaging networks: 5.5–7x (margins, technology, recurring). Accounting (tax/compliance focus): 5–6x EBITDA. Generalist accounting: 3.5–4.5x. Legal (specialised): 4–5.5x. Legal (generalist): 3–4x. Recruitment (specialist): 4.5–5.5x. Staffing (temp/generalist): 2.5–4x.

sections.heading: Strategic positioning for sale
sections.body: If you're planning an exit in healthcare/professional services: (1) Document your client base and retention. This is your #1 negotiating tool. (2) Build a management layer that's not you. If you're the only clinician or practitioner, the business is valued as a job with goodwill, not a platform. (3) Systemise your service delivery. Can a junior clinician deliver 80% of your services with supervision? That's scalability. (4) De-risk single-client concentration. If 30%+ revenue comes from one client, reduce it before you sell — this alone can cost you 1x EBITDA in the valuation. (5) Understand your buyer universe. Are you attractive to regional/national roll-ups? Are there strategic buyers in your sector? Know who's acquiring and why — it shapes your positioning.

title: Tax-Efficient Structuring for Business Owners: NZ vs AU Considerations
excerpt: Holding structures, exit tax planning, AU complications, and CGT strategy for NZ owners with AU exposure.
author: Fairhaven Advisory
intro: October 2025 sits at the halfway point of New Zealand's financial year — and with the April 2025 tax year now six months behind us, owners who haven't yet reviewed their structure are running short on time to make changes before the next year closes. The AU FY26 federal budget, delivered in May, included several updates to small business entity thresholds and cross-border investment rules that have direct relevance for NZ operators with Australian exposure. Tax efficiency is not tax avoidance. It's the difference between structuring a business one way and another, with identical economics but different tax outcomes. This article covers the main options and trade-offs that matter most in the current environment.

sections.heading: NZ sole proprietorship or partnership: the default (often wrong)
sections.body: Many SME owners operate as sole traders or partnerships. It's simple, cheap ($50–200 setup), minimal compliance. Tax: Your business income is assessed at your personal marginal rate (up to 45%). At exit, capital gain is typically tax-free (unless land or financial arrangements). The catch: You have unlimited personal liability for business debts. A liability event (customer sues, contract dispute) can affect personal assets. Most advisers recommend moving out of sole proprietorship once revenue >$500k or risk profile increases. Partnerships are similar: simple but unlimited liability, partners are jointly and severally liable for actions of other partners.

sections.heading: Limited company: the standard play
sections.body: Most NZ SMEs operate as limited companies. Setup: $200–500, annual compliance: $1–3k (depending on complexity). Tax structure: (1) Company earns profit, pays corporate tax (28%). (2) Owner takes dividend, pays personal tax on dividend (up to 45%, reduced by FDR (foreign dividend relief) of 5/19 for most dividends). Net tax: depends on dividend level, but typically 42–45% on profit taken as dividend. At exit: Sale of company shares is tax-free (no capital gains tax in NZ on shares, with limited exceptions). This is huge. If you build a business to $10M value and sell it, the $8M gain is tax-free. But during ownership, income tax is high. Liability: Limited to share capital. Creditors can't pursue personal assets. This is the primary reason to use a company.

sections.heading: Multi-layer structures: holding company + operating company
sections.body: Many larger businesses use a two-layer structure: (1) Operating Company: Runs the business, earns profit, pays tax (28%). (2) Holding Company: Owns the operating company, receives dividends from operating company (tax-free if intercompany), distributes to owner. Why? (1) Asset protection. The holding company owns the operating company as an asset. If something goes wrong operationally, liability is isolated. (2) Exit flexibility. You can sell just the operating company (full sale) or the holding company (full exit). Different tax and transaction costs. (3) Dividend streaming. Allows more flexibility in how profits flow and are taxed at different levels. At exit: If you sell the operating company, the holding company retains the cash and any other assets. If you sell the holding company shares, you exit cleanly. Tax: More complex than single company, but if structured well, more flexible. Setup cost: $500–1,500. Annual compliance: $2–5k (double compliance).

sections.heading: Australian operations: the complication
sections.body: If you have an AU subsidiary or AU operations: AU tax residency kicks in. Your AU entity is taxed in AU on AU-source income (45% corporate tax, 47% top personal rate). If your NZ holding company receives dividends from the AU entity, those dividends are taxed in NZ (usually with some foreign investment fund tax relief, but it's complicated). Cross-border transactions (inter-company charges, royalties, management fees) are scrutinised by tax authorities. Transfer pricing rules (pricing transactions between NZ and AU entities) require documentation. If not documented properly, authorities can adjust prices, creating double taxation. The takeaway: Operating in both NZ and AU requires professional tax planning. This is where generic advice breaks down. You need a tax adviser familiar with both jurisdictions.

sections.heading: Exit tax planning: the critical phase
sections.body: Most owners don't think about exit tax until they're selling. By then, options are limited. Better approach: Plan the exit structure 12–24 months before you sell. Key decisions: (1) What will you sell? (Full company, assets, or a portion?). (2) Will any proceeds stay in a holding company (reinvestment, cash buffer) or come out as personal cash? (3) Are you staying involved post-exit (earn-out, consulting)? Each decision has tax implications. Example: If you sell your operating company for $5M, and the buyer wants the real estate too, you might separate the real estate into a separate entity and lease it to the buyer (passive income, capital preservation). Or you might sell everything and reinvest in a holding company (fresh start, clean exit). These aren't routine decisions; they need planning.

sections.heading: Capital gains planning for NZ business owners
sections.body: NZ doesn't have capital gains tax on shares (with exceptions for dealers, financial arrangements, land, or short-term speculation). But if you're in the business of buying and selling businesses, the Commissioner may argue your gains are income, not capital. There's also the bright-line test for real estate (1–5 year holds). Most SME operators are safe: You're building one business long-term. Selling it is a capital event, not a profit-making scheme. But if you're a serial acquirer or property speculator, tax authority scrutiny increases. The lesson: Be clear about intent. If you're building a business to exit once, not multiple times, document that intent. It supports the capital gains tax-free position.

sections.heading: Retirement planning: KiwiSaver and structuring
sections.body: Many SME owners neglect KiwiSaver because they're in a company structure and KiwiSaver employer contribution is limited ($3,720/year cap). But KiwiSaver is tax-efficient for both employee and employer contributions. Strategies: (1) Max out employer contributions (cheaper than paying the owner as salary + KiwiSaver by the owner). (2) Use KiwiSaver as a long-term exit strategy: accumulate to retirement, live off KiwiSaver + other investments. (3) If exiting at age 55–60, consider timing to minimise personal income tax during the exit (e.g., exit year might be high income; structuring proceeds to defer some to following years can reduce tax). This requires coordination with accountant and financial planner.

sections.heading: NZ vs AU tax efficiency: the comparison
sections.body: NZ: Capital gains on shares are tax-free (huge advantage). Corporate tax 28% (competitive). Personal rates up to 45% (high). AU: Capital gains tax on shares is 50% discount if held >1 year (better than NZ rate if you're in 47% bracket, worse if you're in lower bracket). Corporate tax 30% (slightly higher than NZ). Personal rates up to 47% (slightly higher). Dividend tax: Both NZ and AU have franking/imputation systems (you get credit for company tax paid). Overall: NZ has slight tax advantage for business exit (zero CGT on shares). AU has slight advantage during ownership if you take dividends (franking provides credit). For a NZ owner with AU operations, the structuring trade-off is usually: build in NZ (tax-efficient during ownership), sell NZ company (no CGT), and reinvest in AU if desired.

sections.heading: Action steps: tax-efficient structuring
sections.body: If you're currently a sole trader or partnership: Migrate to a company structure. Cost ~$200–500 now; benefit is unlimited liability protection and better exit optionality. If you're in a company and planning exit >12 months out: Engage a tax adviser to plan the exit structure. Decisions now (e.g., separate real estate, create holding company) are cheaper than changes during the sale. If you have AU operations: Get professional tax advice on holding structure. Transfer pricing documentation must be in place. If you're planning AIP or investor visa: Structure your investments in a holding company that satisfies INZ active involvement requirements. Single-purpose entities for each investment may be necessary. If you're a serial operator (multiple businesses over time): Document your intent for each. Each business should be clearly separate with clear entry/exit documentation. This supports the 'capital gain' (not 'income') tax position.

title: Private Equity in Australasia: What Acquirers Actually Look For
excerpt: PE acquisition thesis, diligence focus, deal structure expectations, and integration planning. What serious buyers care about.
author: Fairhaven Advisory
intro: September 2025 closes out Q3 — and with PE firms finishing their mid-year portfolio reviews, capital deployment mandates for H2 are being confirmed. Funds that had been on the sidelines through 2024's rate uncertainty are now actively scanning for acquisition targets. Private equity doesn't acquire the same way strategic buyers do: PE has a thesis, a playbook, and a hard timeline tied to fund cycle. With H2 deal flow opening up and competition for quality Australasian assets increasing through September, understanding how PE thinks is critical whether you're selling to PE or trying to attract PE interest before the end-of-year window.

sections.heading: The PE thesis: value creation timelines
sections.body: PE firms buy to improve and sell. Typical timeline: 3–5 years. They're not buying forever; they're buying to own, improve, and return capital to their fund investors. The thesis determines what they buy. Typical PE playbooks: (1) Operational improvement. Buy a distressed or under-optimised business. Improve margins, reduce costs, systemise. Exit at higher multiple. Example: a hospitality business with 5% margins that PE improves to 10%, then sells at same multiple but higher absolute profit. (2) Bolt-on roll-up. Buy a platform, then acquire 3–5 smaller businesses in the same sector. Realise synergies. Exit as a larger, more valuable platform. Example: one dental clinic becomes a 5-clinic network over 3 years. (3) Growth and scale. Buy a growing business, invest in sales and marketing, expand product lines, enter new geographies. Exit on higher revenue and growth trajectory. Example: a NZD 5M revenue SaaS business becomes NZD 15M+ in 3 years. (4) Financial engineering. Buy a stable, cash-generative business. Use the cash to pay down debt. Exit at the same multiple but with better leverage profile. Less common in current market, but happens. Each thesis shapes what PE looks for.

sections.heading: What PE actually cares about in diligence
sections.body: Customer concentration and retention: Is 30%+ revenue from one customer? If yes, PE is nervous. Can they improve retention? How? Customer acquisition cost: Are you acquiring customers efficiently? Do your payback timelines make sense? If CAC is 18 months and customer lifetime is 3 years, that's risky. Management team: Who runs this business? Are they PE-ready (i.e., can they execute a scaling or improvement thesis)? Or do they need replacement? PE budgets 20–30% of their value creation timeline to management changes, and it's expensive. Margins and cost structure: Where are the costs? Can they be reduced? Is the cost structure scalable, or does it add linearly with revenue? Growth trajectory: Is the business in a growth market? If revenue is flat, PE needs a clear thesis for how they'll drive growth. They don't buy flat businesses unless there's a strong operational fix. Unit economics: For SaaS or subscription: CAC, LTV, churn rate, NRR. For e-commerce: COGS, shipping costs, return rate, repeat purchase rate. For services: project profitability, staff utilisation. PE digs into the unit economics and models different scenarios. Systems and processes: Can the business scale without the owner? Are processes documented? Is there a management layer? This shapes how much PE budgets for operational investment. Cash flow: How much cash does the business actually generate (not EBITDA; actual cash). Are there working capital surprises (seasonality, customer payment terms)? PE models out 5-year cash to understand what they can return to the fund.

sections.heading: The red flags PE will walk away from
sections.body: Single-customer concentration (>50% revenue): Too much risk. Unless the customer is government or highly stable, PE walks. Revenue decline for 2+ consecutive years: PE buys growth or stability. Declining businesses are a value-destruction bet, not a value-creation one. Founder dependency with no transition plan: If the founder is the business and won't stay post-acquisition, PE is nervous. They budget time and cost for transition, but if it's unclear, they move on. Undocumented processes and unknown costs: If due diligence is opaque, PE assumes worst-case. They'll discount 20–30% as 'operational risk.' Regulatory or compliance issues: If there's litigation, environmental liability, or an audit issue, PE will either walk or discount heavily. Customer acquisition is unpredictable or expensive: If you can't explain how you acquire customers or your CAC is >40% of LTV, PE is skeptical of growth thesis. Products/services that are commoditised: If you compete purely on price with 10+ competitors, PE sees low differentiation and margin pressure. They'll lower their acquisition multiple.

sections.heading: Deal structure: what PE expects
sections.body: Purchase price: Typically 70–80% of deal value at close. 20–30% is deferred as earn-out (usually 24 months) or seller note. This is insurance against misrepresentation. Reps and warranties insurance: Seller indemnifies PE for 12–18 months against misrepresentation of facts. This is standard and relatively cheap (0.5–1% of purchase price). Management retention: PE expects the existing management team to stay for 12–24 months post-close to ensure continuity. Founder transition: If the founder is staying, PE expects a documented transition plan (founder steps out of day-to-day over 6–12 months, for example). If the founder is leaving, PE budgets for management replacement and integration. Earnout mechanics: Usually tied to EBITDA targets or growth targets. If the business underperforms, seller receives less deferred consideration. This aligns seller and PE interests but adds risk to seller. Debt: PE usually brings debt financing (40–50% of purchase price from lenders). This is ABL (asset-based lending) or term loan. Seller doesn't carry this, but debt servicing comes from business cash flow, which affects distributions to PE.

sections.heading: Integration playbook
sections.body: Day 1–30: 'Honeymoon' phase. PE, founders, and management align on priorities. No major changes. Days 30–90: Deep-dive on cost structure and processes. PE starts identifying quick wins. Months 3–6: Implement changes (cost reduction, process improvement). Months 6–12: Scale improvements, assess management team. Who's staying? Who needs replacement? Months 12–24: Growth phase. Once cost is stable, PE invests in sales and marketing or new product development. Months 24–36: Prepare for exit. PE prepares for IPO, trade sale, or secondary buyout. This timeline is rough; PE adjusts based on the specific thesis. But the pattern is: stabilise, improve, scale, exit.

sections.heading: Selling to PE: how to position yourself
sections.body: Tell a clear story about value creation. Why would PE be interested? What's the thesis? Is it operational improvement? Roll-up play? Growth and scale? Make it explicit. Highlight the market opportunity and your position. PE cares about tailwind (is the sector growing?). Strong unit economics. If you have them, lead with them. Historical financials and forward guidance. PE models out 5 years; give them clean data to model. Customer concentration and retention data. This is critical. Be transparent about concentration and provide retention data. Management depth. If you have a capable COO or GM, emphasise it. PE is buying a team, not just a business. Growth levers. What's not yet exploited? Where can PE invest to drive growth? A clear answer builds confidence.

sections.heading: PE timing and process
sections.body: Exclusive Process: You work with a PE firm (or a few firms) in a structured exclusive period. Usually 4–8 weeks. Parallel or Competitive Process: You shop the business to multiple PE firms simultaneously. Takes longer (8–12 weeks) but generates competitive tension and higher valuations. Blind Auction: Investment bank runs the process. Many bidders, no exclusivity, shortest timeline (6–8 weeks) but highest competition. For most NZ businesses, blind auction is overblown — there aren't enough local PE buyers. Exclusive or parallel process is more realistic. Expect 8–16 weeks from first contact to term sheet. Then 4–8 weeks of negotiation. Due diligence happens in parallel, adds 6–8 weeks. Total deal cycle: 18–24 weeks from initial approach to close, assuming no surprises.

sections.heading: What NZ businesses get wrong when pitching PE
sections.body: They focus on what the business is, not what PE can build. 'We're a $3M revenue consulting firm' means nothing. 'We're a $3M revenue consulting firm with a proprietary framework, 40% growth, 5-clinic opportunities in the next 2 years' is a PE story. They underestimate customer concentration risk. 'Our top customer is 25% of revenue' should be accompanied by a plan to reduce that risk. Without it, PE discounts 1x EBITDA. They overestimate margins. PE will verify EBITDA independently. Don't try to hide costs; PE will find them. They focus on past and present, not future. PE cares about what they can build. Talk about market opportunity and growth strategy. They don't understand PE motivations. Founders sometimes think PE is buying them. No — PE is buying a business with upside. Align with that.

title: Regional Opportunities Beyond Auckland: Infrastructure & Population Plays
excerpt: Population shifts, infrastructure investment, and emerging growth hubs outside Auckland. Where to build and acquire for 2025–2026.
author: Fairhaven Advisory
intro: August 2025: the Waikato Expressway is nearing a key construction milestone, Bay of Plenty port expansion tenders have closed, and NZ's secondary cities are quietly outpacing Auckland in population growth. For a decade, capital in NZ flowed to Auckland. That dynamic is now shifting — and mid-winter is when patient investors do their research before the spring deal season opens. Population growth, infrastructure completion timelines, and housing constraints outside Auckland are creating a closing window of opportunity for acquirers and builders who move before the post-completion valuation reset arrives. This article maps the regional plays and what makes them compelling right now.

sections.heading: The population shift is real
sections.body: NZ's population growth is increasingly concentrated in secondary cities. Waikato (Hamilton, Cambridge): +2% YoY, led by people priced out of Auckland seeking lower housing costs. Still close enough for AU flights and business travel. Bay of Plenty (Tauranga): +2.5% YoY, driven by retirees and young families. Emerging as a regional hub. Manawatū (Palmerston North): +1.5% YoY, but more volatile. Otago (Dunedin): +0.8% YoY, but growing from a smaller base. This matters because population drives consumption, employment, and business growth. Businesses in these regions are growing at 2x+ the rate of Auckland peers.

sections.heading: Infrastructure tailwinds
sections.body: Waikato Expressway: Major national investment, completion expected mid-2026. Reduces Auckland-Hamilton commute from 2.5 hrs to <2 hrs. Will transform Hamilton as an Auckland commuter town and distribution hub. Expected impact: 15%+ boost to logistics and hospitality. Bay of Plenty rail: Upgrade and port expansion underway. Makes Tauranga more competitive for export-import logistics. Dunedin: University-led biotech and innovation hub receiving government funding. Creates professional services and tech opportunity. These infrastructure events create investment windows — often 12–18 months before completion when property and business multiples are still reasonable, then 12–18 months after when valuations have already priced in the benefit. We are in that pre-completion window now.

sections.heading: Sector opportunities by region
sections.body: Waikato: Distribution and logistics (growth + Expressway tailwind). Hospitality (visitor economy growth). Light manufacturing (lower rents than Auckland, still close to AU port). Healthcare services (aging population + growth). Healthcare is the most reliable regional play across all secondary cities because aging is structural, not cyclical. Bay of Plenty: Hospitality and tourism (Tauranga as a holiday destination). Logistics (port expansion). Retiree-focused services (financial advice, healthcare). Otago: Education and professional services (university). Biotech and life sciences (government-backed hubs). Manawatū: Diversified plays (smaller population base, fewer tailwinds). Agriculture-adjacent businesses (downstream processing, supply).

sections.heading: Valuation arbitrage
sections.body: Regional businesses trade at 15–30% discount to Auckland peers, even with equivalent profitability and growth. Why? Perceived smaller market, higher integration risk for AU buyers, lower buyer density. But if you acquire a $1M EBITDA business in Hamilton at 4.5x (NZD 4.5M), improve it with systems and management depth, and sell it post-Expressway completion, you might exit at 5.5–6x (NZD 5.5–6M). Add population growth, and you've captured both multiple expansion and earnings growth. This is the regional opportunity: buy at a discount, invest in systemisation and growth, and sell into a strengthening market.

sections.heading: Buyer landscape
sections.body: Regional PE: Smaller funds focused on secondary city roll-ups (not enough deal flow for national PE). Often NZ-based or AU-based with regional focus. Less competitive bid processes than Auckland. Trade buyers: Often local operators looking to roll up in their region or consolidate with peers. Can move faster than institutional buyers. AU strategic buyers: Increasingly looking at regional expansion in NZ. Healthcare networks are the exception — they're attracting capital because demographic tailwind is national.

sections.heading: How to play it
sections.body: As an acquirer: (1) Target secondary cities with infrastructure tailwinds 12–18 months before completion. Multiples are still reasonable — and we're in that window today. (2) Focus on sectors with structural growth (healthcare, logistics, professional services). (3) Plan a 3–5 year hold to capture multiple expansion post-infrastructure. As a seller: (1) Position your regional business as a platform play — not a standalone, but a core asset in a regional roll-up. (2) Emphasise population growth and infrastructure timing. (3) Make your local expertise and customer relationships explicit. (4) Expect to accept slightly lower multiples than Auckland, but plan a faster exit because buyer density is lower (less competition for your deal).

title: Why NZ Businesses Command Exit Multiples: Labour Costs as Competitive Moat
excerpt: Labour arbitrage has ended. NZ SMEs now compete on operational excellence. Why this shifts the exit multiple narrative and what acquirers actually value.
author: Fairhaven Advisory
intro: July 2025 opens the new Australian financial year — and with FY26 budgets freshly set across the Tasman, acquirers are actively benchmarking NZ targets against AU alternatives. The labour cost arbitrage that sustained NZ SME pricing for two decades is rapidly closing. Wage convergence and rising AU competition means NZ businesses can no longer trade on labour savings alone. What replaces it? Operational maturity, systems, and defensible customer moats. As AU-based buyers return from their EOFY reviews with capital to deploy, this article explores what's actually shifted in how they value NZ businesses — and why the July window is the right time to audit your exit readiness.

sections.heading: The labour cost arbitrage is ending
sections.body: Ten years ago, a NZ SME could undercut AU competitors on cost alone. A manufacturing or service business with $2M revenue could compete on price, and the labour savings covered thin margins. Wage inflation in NZ, combined with AU businesses investing in automation and systems, has closed that gap. An equivalent Australian business is no longer 20–30% more expensive to operate. The delta has shrunk to 5–10%, and it's still narrowing. This matters because many NZ business owners have built pricing models and customer relationships on cost leadership. When that moat crumbles, the business is suddenly exposed.

sections.heading: What acquirers now actually value in NZ SMEs
sections.body: The exit multiples of the best NZ businesses haven't collapsed. They've actually improved, but for different reasons. Acquirers now pay for: (1) Systems and repeatability. Can the business run without the owner? Is the playbook documented and transferable? (2) Customer longevity. What's the retention rate? Are relationships transactional or structural? (3) Defensibility. Does the business have a genuine moat — proprietary processes, technical know-how, exclusive partnerships — or is it just operational excellence? (4) Team depth. Is there a management layer below the owner? Can the acquirer integrate this business into a larger platform without losing key people? Labour cost advantage was simple: someone could buy the business, take the margin difference, and pocket it for 3–5 years. Modern acquirers don't work that way. They're looking for sustainable, scalable platforms. NZ businesses that have built those command 6–8x multiples, AU equivalent or better.

sections.heading: How this changes exit strategy
sections.body: If your business strategy has been 'stay lean, undercut on price, grow revenue,' you need to shift before you exit. Build systems. Document processes. Create a management layer that isn't you. Invest in customer retention, not just acquisition. These moves are expensive and time-consuming, but they're what modern acquirers actually pay for. A $3M revenue business with a COO, systems in place, and 85%+ customer retention will exit at 7–8x EBITDA. The same revenue business that's still owner-dependent, with thin margins and transactional customers, might only pull 3–4x, or worse, not attract serious acquirers at all. The good news: systemisation creates immediate operational benefit. You don't just exit better — you run a better business in the meantime.

sections.heading: The regional play: why NZ businesses can still command a premium
sections.body: Despite wage convergence, NZ businesses can still be more valuable to the right acquirer. Reasons: (1) Market access. A NZ business with established supplier relationships and customer networks in the Pacific region has something an AU business doesn't. (2) Regulatory familiarity. Commerce Act compliance, FMA registration, health & safety requirements — these are different in NZ, and an AU acquirer needs someone who knows the playbook. (3) Founder reputation. If you're a known operator in NZ with a track record, your business carries brand value that doesn't translate to spreadsheets but absolutely influences valuation. (4) Talent stability. An AU acquirer integrating a NZ business keeps the management team in place — that's worth a 0.5–1x EBITDA premium because integration risk drops. Play these cards explicitly in exit planning. Make your regulatory expertise, market relationships, and team stickiness visible to potential acquirers.

sections.heading: Preparing for 2026 exits
sections.body: If you're planning an exit in the next 12–18 months, here's what matters now: (1) Get a business valuer to benchmark your EBITDA and multiples against recent comparable exits (not asking prices, actual exits). (2) Commission a systems audit: where is the business dependent on you? Can those dependencies be removed? (3) Stress-test customer retention. Run a churn analysis for the last 3 years. If retention is below 80%, make it a priority before exit — this is the #1 red flag acquirers see. (4) Build management depth. Hire or promote a CFO or COO in the 18 months before exit. This move alone can add 1–1.5x EBITDA to the purchase price. (5) Get serious about acquirer targeting. Who's actually likely to buy your business, and why? Build that story explicitly — it drives the conversation and the multiple.
